nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas of shallow lagoon reefs and coastal bays on silty slopes, recorded across a depth range of 5 to 88 m, though most individuals are found between 5 and 20 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Sand and rubble flats adjacent to reefs, usually as monogamous pairs sharing a burrow.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty foods: mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, blackworms, and quality prepared carnivore foods. As a dedicated sand-sifter — a fish that spends much of its day picking through the substrate for small invertebrates and organic matter — it benefits from established live sand and an attached refugium to support the frequent small meals it naturally takes.

avoidWith: boisterous tankmates that outcompete it for food

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3311x2483. Tack-sharp in-situ shot on sand, whole fish in frame; clearly shows pale body, dark brown vertical bars, black eyespot on first dorsal fin and blue cheek stripe.
Ewout Knoester; 1024x683. Sharp side profile on sand showing bars, dorsal eyespot and blue cheek stripe; good vibrant colour, slightly lower resolution.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1536. Sharp, vibrant individual with reddish-brown bars, black dorsal eyespot and blue cheek stripe; minor corner watermark.

summary: The Tiger Watchman Goby is a pale-bodied sand-sifting sleeper goby marked with three dark brown bars and a black eyespot on the first dorsal fin. Generally peaceful toward other species but territorial against its own kind unless kept as a bonded pair; requires a deep fine sand bed.
careNotes: The Tiger Watchman Goby is a sand-sifting sleeper goby that continuously mouths and screens the substrate for small invertebrates and detritus, which helps keep the sand bed turned over but can deplete its microfauna over time. A fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep is generally advised, and a mature tank with an attached refugium — a small separate compartment where live organisms can reproduce undisturbed — is commonly recommended to help replenish what the goby picks out. Frequent small feedings are important because individuals can lose condition if outcompeted at mealtimes. Tankmates should be peaceful; conspecifics and other sleeper gobies tend to fight unless introduced as a known male-female pair. A tightly fitting lid is strongly recommended because Valenciennea species have been known to jump when startled or harassed.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity on rare occasions when a bonded pair is established; eggs are typically laid in the pair's burrow. Larval rearing is considered very difficult and is not routinely accomplished by hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet: 5 in max, 30 gal min, easy care level, peaceful but territorial with conspecifics, reef safe, carnivore, pH 8.1–8.4, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, range, and habitat: max 15 cm SL, depth 5–88 m (typ. 5–20 m), preferred temperature 22–27°C, Indo-Pacific (Indian Ocean to western tropical Pacific), sandy areas of shallow lagoon reefs, lives in pairs. IUCN Least Concern. Tank minimum biased larger than LiveAquaria's 30 gal figure (rounded to 150 L) per the husbandry-caution rule given the species' need for swimming space and sand-bed footprint.

fish.breedingNotes: Bonded pairs have been reported to spawn in their shared burrow; larvae are very difficult to rear and captive-bred individuals are uncommon in the trade.
